institution: Sector-wide typology - corporate payments / cross-border wires
source_documents: https://www.ic3.gov (FBI IC3 - BEC PSAs and Annual Report)
risk_signals: A last-minute change of a vendor's or executive's bank account details requested over email
Lookalike or spoofed sender domain, often with urgency and a request for secrecy
Payment instruction that bypasses the normal approval or verification workflow
analyst_reasoning: Attackers compromise or spoof a trusted email account (executive or supplier) and redirect an otherwise-legitimate payment to a fraud account. The fraud rides on real business context, so the tells are the channel (email-only), the change (bank details), and the pressure (urgency/secrecy) rather than any technical anomaly.
investigation_steps: Verify the account-change request through an independent, pre-established channel (a known phone number)
Analyse email headers and the sender domain for spoofing or lookalike registration
Check whether the payment skipped standard dual-approval or vendor-master controls
decision: Halt the payment, confirm details out-of-band with the counterparty, and if funds have moved, request a recall and file with IC3/authorities immediately.
outcome: Redirected wires are frequently large and cross-border; recovery depends on same-day recall requests before funds are withdrawn.
lessons_learned: Any emailed change of bank details must be verified out-of-band before payment
Urgency plus secrecy plus a payment change is the BEC signature
Vendor-master and dual-approval controls exist precisely to stop single-channel redirection
